questions.question: Why should market-data fanout be separated from the matching engine core?
options: Because downstream subscriber lag and replay demand should not directly slow execution latency.
Because market data never needs durability once sent.
Because subscribers can always tolerate stale data equally.
Because filtering is impossible in asynchronous systems.
questions.explanation: The fanout layer is a major source of variable downstream pressure.

questions.question: What is the main reason to define subscriber tiers explicitly?
options: Different subscribers have different latency, replay, and cost expectations that should shape policy.
Tiering automatically removes the need for quota enforcement.
All subscribers can be served from one identical backlog budget safely.
Tiering is only a billing decision and not an architecture concern.
questions.explanation: Tiering is operational policy, not only pricing.

questions.question: What signal best shows that one subscriber is becoming a shared-cost problem?
options: Its lag, retained-by-subscriber bytes, and replay traffic relative to its tier budget.
The age of the subscriber's account manager's laptop.
The count of markdown files in the team runbook directory.
The average CPU temperature on developer workstations.
questions.explanation: Lag and retained state reveal the real shared-platform pressure.

questions.question: Why isolate replay traffic from live delivery lanes?
options: Replay is bursty and can otherwise starve low-latency live fanout.
Replay must always be globally ordered with execution across all symbols.
Live feeds do not require storage once relays are running.
Isolation guarantees every subscriber sees identical delay.
questions.explanation: Replay and live traffic have very different latency profiles and resource shapes.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with precomputing common symbol bundles for subscribers?
options: It lowers per-request filter cost but adds maintenance and storage complexity.
It eliminates the need for relay failover.
It makes tier-specific quotas impossible to enforce.
It guarantees zero lag for all replay requests.
questions.explanation: Precomputation saves CPU later by paying complexity earlier.

questions.question: What is the most credible response to a regional relay outage followed by mass reconnects?
options: Stagger reconnects with jitter and allow multi-relay failover instead of accepting an instant reconnect storm.
Force every client to reconnect simultaneously to rebuild order faster.
Disable quotas until all sessions are back.
Drop all replay support permanently for every tier.
questions.explanation: Reconnect storms need admission shaping, not a thundering herd.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals a filtering bottleneck?
options: Filter CPU cost and per-product delivery lag rising faster than raw feed ingress.
The number of market holidays in the current quarter.
The ratio of mobile to desktop clients in the corporate intranet.
The count of feed documentation PDFs downloaded last month.
questions.explanation: Filtering cost should be measured directly on the delivery plane.

questions.question: Which redesign is most likely if premium subscribers demand much larger symbol sets than planned?
options: Revisit filter placement, bundle popular symbol groups, and tighten quota or pricing around extreme fanout cost.
Move live distribution back into the matching process.
Stop tracking subscriber lag so the problem stays invisible.
Replace regional relays with one central feed server.
questions.explanation: Extreme symbol fanout changes both the technical and product policy shape.
